metadata.summary: Three-pillar SEO framework: Technical (crawlability), On-Page (content intent), Off-Page (backlinks). Covers audits, content optimization, link building, and growth metrics.

content.title: SEO Fundamentals
content.summary: The three-pillar SEO framework — Technical (crawlability), On-Page (content and intent match), Off-Page (backlinks and trust) — covering audit checklists, content optimization by search intent, link building strategy overview, keyword research approach, and key metrics for organic growth.

why.p: 53% of all website traffic comes from organic search. Without SEO, content is invisible regardless of quality. Most SEO failures trace to one missing pillar: a technically broken site that can't be indexed, content that mismatches search intent, or zero external authority signals. Understanding all three pillars prevents wasted effort on the wrong layer.

list.item: Starting SEO from scratch on a new project
Diagnosing why a site gets no organic traffic despite publishing content
Setting up a monthly SEO reporting framework
Creating an SEO content brief for a target keyword
Identifying which pillar (technical/on-page/off-page) is the current bottleneck
When you need deep technical SEO implementation detail — use seo-basics
When you need advanced link building tactics — use growth-seo-link-building
For AI search optimization (AEO) — use technical-seo-for-ai or zero-click-search-adaptation
When PPC is the channel, not organic — use ppc-manager

rule.statement: Fix technical SEO blockers before investing in content or link building — a site that cannot be crawled and indexed ranks for nothing regardless of content quality.
rule.rationale: Technical issues (blocked robots.txt, noindex tags, crawl errors) create a ceiling that content and backlinks cannot overcome. The order of operations matters: Technical first, On-Page second, Off-Page third.

rule.statement: Audit crawlability: robots.txt allows important pages (test in Search Console); indexability: key pages appear in site:domain.com search; performance: Core Web Vitals pass (LCP, INP, CLS); mobile: passes Google Mobile-Friendly Test; HTTPS: SSL certificate installed, no mixed content; structure: XML sitemap exists and submitted to Search Console; canonicalization: no duplicate content, correct canonical tags.
rule.rationale: Each of these technical factors is a potential ceiling on ranking performance. A single critical issue (e.g., robots.txt blocking all pages) prevents indexing entirely.

antipattern.reason: This is the single most common technical SEO disaster — frequently introduced during development and left deployed. Results in zero indexed pages. Always verify robots.txt before and after any site launch.

rule.statement: Match search intent before optimizing for keywords — content that mismatches what the searcher wants will not rank regardless of keyword density.
rule.rationale: Google's ranking objective is user satisfaction, not keyword matching. Informational intent queries need guides/explainers; commercial intent needs comparisons/reviews; transactional intent needs product pages. Intent mismatch causes high bounce rates that signal low quality.

rule.statement: Optimize on-page elements: Title tag: primary keyword + benefit, under 60 characters; Meta description: compelling summary, 120-160 characters; URL: short, keyword-rich, hyphen-separated; H1: single H1 containing primary keyword; Content: covers topic completely, beats competitors in depth; Internal links: link to related pages with descriptive anchor text; External links: cite authoritative sources (signals trustworthiness); Schema markup: Article, Organization, FAQ as appropriate.
rule.rationale: These elements collectively signal to Google what the page is about and demonstrate authority and relevance to the user.

rule.statement: Prioritize link quality over quantity: one link from a DR 60 relevant site outweighs 50 links from DR 20 irrelevant directories.
rule.rationale: Google's link quality assessment considers domain authority, relevance, anchor text naturalness, and link placement. High-volume low-quality link building can trigger algorithmic or manual penalties.

rule.statement: Build links through: Guest posting on relevant, high-authority publications; Resource link building: resource pages in your niche; HARO / journalist queries: inbound high-authority links; Digital PR and original research: links from media coverage. Avoid: buying links, link farms, excessive reciprocal linking.
rule.rationale: Quality link sources build domain authority and topical relevance. Low-quality tactics risk algorithmic penalties and waste time.

rule.statement: Identify search intent before writing content — create the format that matches what the searcher expects to find.
rule.rationale: A page targeting "best project management tools" that is structured as a how-to guide (not a comparison) will rank poorly even with perfect keyword optimization, because the format mismatches the informational + commercial intent behind the query.

rule.statement: Classify intent: Informational: how-to guides, explainers, definitions ("what is RAG"); Commercial investigation: comparisons, reviews, "best X" lists; Transactional: product pages, pricing pages, sign-up flows; Navigational: brand-specific searches — optimize for brand terms.
rule.rationale: Each intent type requires a different content format and positioning. Format mismatch is a primary cause of low rankings despite good keyword optimization.

rule.statement: Cover the topic completely — include all subtopics and related questions that top-ranking competitors cover, plus at least one angle they do not.
rule.rationale: Google's ranking assesses comprehensiveness relative to competing pages. A page missing sections that all competitors include will be ranked lower as less authoritative on the topic.

rule.statement: Analyze top 3 SERP results: word count, sections covered, questions answered. Extract "People Also Ask" questions and answer them in the article. Cover edge cases and advanced scenarios that thin content ignores. Add unique value: original research, first-hand experience, contrarian angle.
rule.rationale: Competitive analysis and comprehensiveness are direct ranking factors. Unique value differentiates your page and builds links/shares.

rule.statement: Update outdated content with new information and change the "last updated" date — do not publish duplicate new articles when a refresh would serve the same purpose.
rule.rationale: Google rewards freshness for queries where recency matters. A refreshed authoritative article with existing backlinks outperforms a new article with no backlinks targeting the same keyword.

antipattern.description: Keyword stuffing — repeating the target keyword unnaturally throughout the content
antipattern.reason: Google penalizes keyword stuffing as a spam signal. Semantic search understands topical relevance without keyword repetition; natural language performs better.

antipattern.description: Targeting high-volume keywords without assessing difficulty
antipattern.reason: New or low-authority sites cannot rank for competitive head terms. Starting with long-tail keywords (lower volume, lower difficulty) builds authority that eventually enables ranking for head terms.
New content takes 3-6 months to rank; authority building takes 12+ months. Abandoning SEO due to no visible results at 30 days is the most common reason SEO investments fail.

rule.statement: Track these metrics monthly: Organic traffic: growing month-over-month; Keywords in top 10: increasing count; Domain authority: growing over 6-12 month horizon; Core Web Vitals: all green in Search Console; Click-through rate: above 3% for organic listings.
rule.rationale: These metrics indicate whether SEO efforts are working across all three pillars. Stagnation in any metric signals a bottleneck to investigate.

template.purpose: SEO content brief: keyword data, SERP analysis, content requirements, on-page elements
Monthly SEO report template with traffic, keyword movement, backlinks, actions
